author: @jeffcampbell1706
text: Can you do several in series or parallel?

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Yes, you can.

author: @TrollFaceTheMan
text: What I'd imagine the issues might be is that PC fans need to spin normally fast to get unusable power. Most water sources though I Imagine are going to be more lower speeds and higher torque which won't be good for direct driving a PC Fan at the speeds it needs.

replies.author: @Barskor1
replies.text: Solution, find the area with the greatest incline get the largest diameter pipe you can run it down the incline aways connect a smaller diameter pipe run it aways repeat and then add the fan or other generator.

replies.author: @TrollFaceTheMan
replies.text: @Barskor1, it's definitely an option. Trying to build up higher pressure to spin faster but also for most applications using a narrow fast moving stream of water you want a different cup design and angle of force. Like a Pelton Cup Wheel.

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: In Northern New York state, the canal system used to provide pretty good water pressure to everyone who lived nearby.  A factory near where my great grandparents lived made water-powered dynamos that would generate a small amount of electricity from water piped from the canal.  I think people used them for household and small shop electricity right up until the 1920s.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text:
I wonder how long ProLong spray would work, as it is demonstrated as spraying into distributor caps that if they get a drop of water in them foul up terribly but you can't block the metal on metal contacts or they fail to work too, and these guys after spraying it with the ProLong, filled the cap full of water and slapped it on the assembly, then fired things up and it worked like it was perfectly bone dry.

Only issue is I am not sure if it is meant to last long term or if it is only meant for intermittent drenching, and long term water attacking it might wash it away? no idea, but its the successor to super lubricant additives over DuraLube because it also does not when it runs off eventually, rust the hell out of the metal like DuraLube because it's not made with a nitrogen bonding agent as DuraLube was, so there is nothing left to act as a catalyst to oxidizing the iron in the engine LOL.

author: @totherarf
text:
Why bother insulating it?
At that kind of voltage the resistivity of water (2.5M ohms/ meter) would not give much leakage.

replies.author: @dansw0rkshop
replies.text: And really the only thing needing waterproofing is the solder joints.  Everything else is already insulated i.e. the magnet wire.  If it's a ball bearing fan, those need waterproofing more than the electrics IMO.

replies.author: @DimitriPappas
replies.text: @dansw0rkshop most of these pc fans tend to be brushless DC AFAIK, so no ball bearings

replies.author: @dansw0rkshop
replies.text: @DimitriPappas Yes all that I've encountered are brushless, most are bronze bearing, some are ball.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: over time everything rots in water mate including the steel core and the solder joints - my main worry was actually the seal on the bearings

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Good point! I work in the electrics business so I tend to think of that first! ;o)

replies.author: @ChadKovac
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering don't guess man!! This could  mean using bodies of water that have movement but not concentrated movement. Like you can't tap into a overflow or anything you just have the body of water that moves. 

Something like this 12 in x 12 in say could be dropped off of a boat in the ocean off a line to a depth with a vibrating current and generate electricity while sitting still with no wind.. 

Maybe somebody like me with a spring fed pond whose water moves but not rapidly could benefit from a larger system.

Water, being so much more dense than air should be able to create tremendous amounts of raw power. Big, slow moving torque monster!
